nutrient_source.title: Chrysanthemum Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chrysanthemum Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010016, with confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken as-is from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar). GI estimated at 55 (low) by analogy with sweetened milk tea / tea-based beverages with non-dairy creamer and reduced sugar, referencing the University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened tea beverages (GI range 47–65). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (30.6 g from 250 g serving × 12.24 g/100 g) / 100 = 16.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.84 g sugar/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g beverage gate; independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium (10 mg/100 g), the 6.84 g/100 g sugar content classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance; individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit intake and choose genuinely unsweetened alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.84 g sugar/100 g); liquid sugar with rapid glycaemic impact; 'less sugar' variant still triggers SSB gate
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage contains 6.84 g sugar/100 g as a liquid sugar-sweetened drink, which triggers the SSB hard gate for type 2 diabetes management; even 'less sugar' variants are not suitable, and only a genuinely unsweetened version (sugar = 0 g) would be acceptable for individuals managing blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contains palm oil (saturated fat); added sugar contributes to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The non-dairy creamer component likely contributes saturated fat from palm oil, and the added sugar (6.84 g/100 g) may adversely affect lipid profiles; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and verify the creamer ingredient list for hydrogenated oils or trans fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contains palm oil contributing saturated fat and potentially trace trans fat, which are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Non-dairy creamer in this beverage likely contains palm oil and may contain hydrogenated oils; trans fat is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis suitability, and chrysanthemum polyphenols at beverage dilution are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory fat profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular Singaporean floral tea beverage made by brewing dried chrysanthemum flowers (Chrysanthemum morifolium) in hot water and blending the infusion with non-dairy creamer or milk, sweetened with a reduced amount of sugar. The drink has a delicate floral aroma, a mildly sweet and slightly bitter taste, and a light, milky texture. It is commonly sold at local beverage stalls, kopitiam counters, and bubble tea shops across Singapore. The 'less sugar' designation indicates a reduced but non-zero sugar level compared to the standard version; at 6.84 g sugar per 100 g, it still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage under Singapore HPB and clinical nutrition criteria. Nutritionally, it provides 65 kcal per 100 g, with 12.24 g carbohydrate, 1.81 g fat (primarily from non-dairy creamer), negligible protein (0 g as reported by HPB), 1.28 g dietary fibre from chrysanthemum solids, and low sodium at 10 mg/100 g. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification, and the non-dairy creamer component raises concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients due to potential saturated and trans fat content from palm oil. Chrysanthemum has mild antioxidant properties, but at beverage dilution these are insufficient to confer meaningful clinical benefit.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010016) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a canonical Singapore beverage with an exact HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific beverage.
notes: protein_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id D08010016); this is confirmed zero per the approved HPB source, consistent with a dilute tea-based beverage where protein contribution from non-dairy creamer at this dilution is below the reporting threshold — confirmed zero per HPB. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar) preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Note: non-dairy creamer likely contains palm oil; consumers should check product label for trans fat content. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chrysanthemum milk tea (less sugar) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GI of 55 is considered 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >70 is high).
